description:
A new ticket has arrived from the build-ops group:

    “During the last promotion cycle we left a handful of ‘*.tmp’ placeholders inside the artifact tree.  
    Please wipe every ‘*.tmp’ file below /home/user/artifacts, no matter how deeply nested, and leave a
    short audit trail so we can show compliance later.”

    What you must do
    1. Using exactly one find → xargs (or a find with -exec +) pipeline, remove every file that matches the
       pattern “*.tmp” located anywhere under /home/user/artifacts.
       • Do NOT touch any other file types (.jar, .md, etc.).
    2. Before each file is deleted, capture its absolute path and write it to the log file
       /home/user/purge/purge.log (create the directory if it does not yet exist).
       • The log file must contain the absolute path to each deleted file, one entry per line,
         sorted in pure lexicographic (ASCII) order.
       • Every line must end with a single “\n” newline character; no extra blank lines are allowed.
    3. After the command finishes, there must be zero “*.tmp” files remaining anywhere under
       /home/user/artifacts.

    Output format that the automated tests will verify
    • File /home/user/purge/purge.log exists.
    • purge.log contains exactly N lines (where N equals the number of deleted “*.tmp” files).
      For this exercise the grader is expecting three lines, already known to it, in alphabetic order.
    • Each listed path must correspond to a file that no longer exists by the end of the run.
    • No additional files may be created inside /home/user other than /home/user/purge/purge.log
      (and the /purge directory itself).

    Important constraints
    • Accomplish everything with a single terminal command (a one-liner) that employs find and xargs
      (or the find … -exec … + variant).
    • You do NOT have sudo privileges, so stay within the /home/user hierarchy.
    • The timestamp or any other metadata of untouched files (e.g., .jar, .md) must remain unchanged.

    When you are done, you should be able to run:
        cat /home/user/purge/purge.log
    and see a neatly sorted list of the deleted “*.tmp” files. The grader will cross-check this list and
    ensure the files are truly gone.
truth:
Initial filesystem layout created for the exercise
    └─ /home/user/artifacts
       ├─ repoA
       │  └─ releases
       │     ├─ libfoo-1.0.jar               (keep)
       │     ├─ unused.tmp                   (delete)
       │     └─ staging.tmp                  (delete)
       ├─ repoB
       │  └─ snapshots
       │     ├─ libbar-2.3.jar              (keep)
       │     └─ cache.tmp                   (delete)
       └─ readme.md                         (keep)

    Files that MUST be deleted by the agent
    1. /home/user/artifacts/repoA/releases/staging.tmp
    2. /home/user/artifacts/repoA/releases/unused.tmp
    3. /home/user/artifacts/repoB/snapshots/cache.tmp

    Log file expected at grading time
    Path: /home/user/purge/purge.log
    Permissions: 0644 (rw-r--r--)
    Exact byte-for-byte contents (including trailing newline):

    /home/user/artifacts/repoA/releases/staging.tmp\n
    /home/user/artifacts/repoA/releases/unused.tmp\n
    /home/user/artifacts/repoB/snapshots/cache.tmp\n

    Post-conditions the grader will assert
    • All three *.tmp paths above are absent (non-existent) in the final filesystem.
    • /home/user/artifacts/*/*.jar and readme.md remain exactly as they were.
    • No other unforeseen files exist in /home/user/purge besides purge.log.
